does: This paper develops a framework for evaluating biological capabilities in AI models by drawing on established dual-use research oversight from the life sciences. It identifies high-consequence biological risks—particularly those capable of causing pandemic-scale harm—as priority targets for AI safety evaluation. The authors argue that biological capabilities in large language models and other AI systems warrant systematic assessment using principles already developed by biosafety and biosecurity experts over decades of managing risky life sciences research. The work bridges longstanding biological risk management practices with emerging AI safety concerns, proposing that evaluations should focus on capabilities most relevant to creating high-consequence pathogens or enabling dangerous biological engineering rather than treating all biological knowledge queries equally.
connects: This work extends decades of dual-use research oversight in biology—institutional frameworks that scientists and policy professionals developed to manage risks from engineered organisms and pathogen variants—into the AI domain. It responds to rapid increases in AI capabilities and recent government initiatives addressing AI safety and misuse prevention. The paper builds on existing biosafety governance structures and risk assessment methodologies while addressing a newly urgent problem: the concentration of biological knowledge and potential biological capabilities in large AI models. It connects emerging concerns about AI misuse with mature biosecurity thinking, adapting established concepts like dual-use research of concern to evaluate whether and how AI systems might enable biological harm.
enables: This framework enables more targeted and effective safety evaluations of AI systems, allowing researchers to focus biological risk assessment on genuinely high-consequence capabilities rather than all biology-related queries. It points toward developing technical evaluation standards for AI that incorporate expertise from biosecurity and public health communities, creating interdisciplinary governance structures. The work suggests future development of specific benchmarks assessing whether models can provide instructions for pandemic-capable pathogen creation or enable dangerous biological engineering. It enables policy development informed by proven biosafety practices, potentially informing government oversight of AI development and deployment. The framework also points toward better coordination between AI safety research, biological security communities, and policymakers in managing risks from increasingly capable AI systems.
